q: Summarize the book of Hosea: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The prophet Hosea son of Beeri; c. 755–715 B.C., addressing the northern kingdom of Israel in its final decades before Assyria; pre-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** God's covenant love pursues an unfaithful people. Hosea's marriage to faithless Gomer dramatizes the LORD's steadfast love for spiritually adulterous Israel and His resolve to redeem rather than abandon her.

**Outline.**
- 1–3 — Hosea's marriage as a living parable of Israel's harlotry
- 4–7 — Indictment of Israel's idolatry and false worship
- 8–10 — Coming judgment for sown wind, reaped whirlwind
- 11–14 — God's fatherly love and a final call to return

**Christ & significance.** Hosea reveals the heart of a God who loves the undeserving, fulfilled when Christ redeems an adulterous people as His bride. Matthew 2:15 applies "out of Egypt I called my son" to Jesus, and Paul cites Hosea on calling a non-people God's people (Romans 9:25–26).
summary: Hosea, c. 755–715 B.C. to the dying northern kingdom. His marriage to unfaithful Gomer pictures God's relentless covenant love for adulterous Israel.

q: Summarize the book of Joel: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The prophet Joel son of Pethuel; date debated but conservatively often placed early (c. 835 B.C.) or pre-exilic, addressing Judah and Jerusalem.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** The Day of the LORD. A devastating locust plague becomes a summons to repentance and a window onto God's coming judgment and the outpouring of His Spirit.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–20 — A locust plague signals the Day of the LORD
- 2:1–17 — The army of judgment and a call to rend the heart
- 2:18–32 — Restoration and the promise of the Spirit poured out
- 3:1–21 — Judgment on the nations and blessing for God's people

**Christ & significance.** Joel's promise that God will pour out His Spirit on all flesh (2:28–32) is fulfilled at Pentecost, quoted by Peter in Acts 2:16–21. Paul echoes "everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved" (Romans 10:13), pointing to the gospel age inaugurated by Christ.
summary: Joel, likely c. 835 B.C. to Judah. A locust plague heralds the Day of the LORD; God promises to pour out His Spirit, fulfilled at Pentecost.

q: Summarize the book of Amos: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Amos, a shepherd and fig-grower from Tekoa in Judah; c. 760–750 B.C., sent to prophesy against the prosperous northern kingdom of Israel under Jeroboam II; pre-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** God demands justice, not mere ritual. Amos thunders against social oppression and empty religion, warning that covenant privilege brings greater accountability and that judgment is near.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Oracles against the nations, ending with Judah and Israel
- 3–6 — Sermons indicting Israel's injustice and complacency
- 7:1–9:10 — Five visions of impending judgment
- 9:11–15 — Promise to restore the fallen booth of David

**Christ & significance.** Amos's call to "let justice roll down like waters" (5:24) anticipates Christ, the righteous Judge and advocate of the poor. James cites the rebuilding of David's fallen tent (9:11–12) at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:16–17) as fulfilled in the ingathering of the Gentiles.
summary: Amos, a Judean shepherd, c. 760 B.C. to prosperous Israel. He condemns injustice and empty worship, demanding that justice roll down like waters.

q: Summarize the book of Obadiah: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The prophet Obadiah, of whom little is known; likely shortly after Jerusalem's fall in 586 B.C., addressing Edom for its treachery against Judah; exilic period.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** Judgment on Edom and the certainty of the Day of the LORD. Edom's pride and violence against its brother Jacob will be repaid, while the LORD secures His people's deliverance.

**Outline.**
- 1–9 — Edom's pride humbled and its destruction announced
- 10–14 — Edom's violence against brother Jacob indicted
- 15–16 — The Day of the LORD upon all nations
- 17–21 — Deliverance on Mount Zion and the kingdom belongs to the LORD

**Christ & significance.** The shortest Old Testament book closes with "the kingdom shall be the LORD's" (v. 21), anticipating Christ's universal reign. Edom's downfall and Zion's deliverance foreshadow the final triumph of God's people in the Messiah.
summary: Obadiah, c. 586 B.C., against Edom for gloating over Jerusalem's fall. The Old Testament's shortest book ends with the kingdom belonging to the LORD.

q: Summarize the book of Jonah: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Jonah son of Amittai, a prophet of Israel from Gath-hepher (2 Kings 14:25); c. 780–760 B.C., sent to Nineveh, capital of Assyria; pre-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy / Narrative.

**Theme.** God's sovereign mercy extends even to the nations. Through a reluctant prophet, the LORD shows His compassion on repentant Gentiles and rebukes the narrow heart that resents grace.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Jonah flees, the storm, and the swallowing fish
- 2 — Jonah's prayer of thanksgiving from the fish
- 3 — Jonah preaches and Nineveh repents
- 4 — God rebukes Jonah's anger and teaches His compassion

**Christ & significance.** Jesus calls Jonah's three days in the fish a sign of His own death and resurrection (Matthew 12:39–41), and Nineveh's repentance shames the unbelieving. Jonah's mission of mercy to Gentiles foreshadows the gospel going to all nations in Christ.
summary: Jonah, c. 780–760 B.C., the reluctant prophet sent to Nineveh. His three days in the fish prefigure Christ's resurrection and the gospel for the nations.

q: Summarize the book of Micah: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Micah of Moresheth in Judah; c. 735–700 B.C., a contemporary of Isaiah, addressing both Samaria and Jerusalem; pre-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** Judgment and hope held together. Micah denounces the corruption of leaders, prophets, and priests, yet promises a coming Ruler from Bethlehem and a remnant restored by God's pardoning grace.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Judgment on Samaria and Judah for injustice
- 3 — Indictment of corrupt rulers and prophets
- 4–5 — Future glory of Zion and the Ruler from Bethlehem
- 6–7 — God's covenant lawsuit and final hope in His mercy

**Christ & significance.** Micah 5:2 foretells the Messiah's birth in Bethlehem, cited in Matthew 2:6. The famous summons to "do justice, love kindness, and walk humbly" (6:8) and the closing wonder at a pardoning God (7:18–19) find their answer in Christ, who forgives and shepherds His remnant.
summary: Micah, c. 735–700 B.C., contemporary of Isaiah, to Judah and Samaria. He pairs judgment with hope, foretelling the Bethlehem-born Ruler of 5:2.

q: Summarize the book of Nahum: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Nahum the Elkoshite; c. 663–612 B.C. (after the fall of Thebes, before Nineveh's fall), pronouncing judgment on Nineveh and Assyria for the comfort of Judah; pre-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** The certain downfall of Nineveh. The LORD is a jealous, avenging God who is also slow to anger; He will judge the brutal Assyrian empire and so comfort His oppressed people.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–8 — God's majesty and avenging power
- 1:9–15 — Assurance of Nineveh's fall and Judah's relief
- 2 — The siege and sack of Nineveh portrayed
- 3 — Woe to the bloody city and its certain ruin

**Christ & significance.** Nahum proclaims that "the LORD is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble" (1:7) even as He judges evil, displaying the holiness and justice perfected at the cross. The fall of a seemingly invincible oppressor assures God's people that Christ will finally overthrow every hostile power.
summary: Nahum, c. 663–612 B.C., announces the fall of brutal Nineveh. God's vengeance on Assyria brings comfort and deliverance to oppressed Judah.

q: Summarize the book of Habakkuk: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The prophet Habakkuk; c. 640–605 B.C., as Babylon rose to power, addressing Judah on the eve of judgment; pre-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** Faith that wrestles and then worships. Habakkuk questions why God tolerates evil and uses a wicked Babylon to judge Judah, and is answered that the righteous shall live by faith while the proud fall.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–11 — First complaint and God's answer: Babylon is coming
- 1:12–2:5 — Second complaint and the call to live by faith
- 2:6–20 — Five woes against the proud oppressor
- 3 — Habakkuk's prayer and psalm of trembling trust

**Christ & significance.** "The righteous shall live by his faith" (2:4) becomes a cornerstone of the gospel, quoted in Romans 1:17, Galatians 3:11, and Hebrews 10:38 to teach justification by faith. Habakkuk's resolve to rejoice in God though all fails (3:17–18) models faith fulfilled in Christ.
summary: Habakkuk, c. 640–605 B.C., wrestles with God over evil and the rise of Babylon. The answer—"the righteous shall live by faith"—anchors the gospel.

q: Summarize the book of Zephaniah: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Zephaniah, of royal descent (great-great-grandson of Hezekiah); c. 640–621 B.C., during Josiah's reign, addressing Judah and Jerusalem; pre-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** The Day of the LORD brings sweeping judgment and a purified remnant. Zephaniah warns of universal wrath against sin yet promises that a humble remnant will be saved and God will rejoice over His people.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–18 — The great Day of the LORD against Judah and the earth
- 2:1–3:8 — Judgment on the nations and on Jerusalem
- 3:9–13 — A purified remnant gathered and humbled
- 3:14–20 — Songs of joy as God rejoices over His people

**Christ & significance.** Amid threatened judgment, Zephaniah ends with God singing over His redeemed (3:17), a love fulfilled in Christ who saves the meek remnant. The Day of the LORD theme points to Christ's final judgment and the gathering of a people who trust in His name.
summary: Zephaniah, c. 640–621 B.C. under Josiah, proclaims the Day of the LORD against Judah, yet ends with God rejoicing over a saved remnant.

q: Summarize the book of Haggai: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The prophet Haggai; 520 B.C. (precisely dated to the second year of Darius), urging the returned exiles in Jerusalem to finish the temple; post-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** Put God's house first. Haggai rebukes the people's misplaced priorities while their lives languish, calling them to rebuild the temple with the promise of God's presence and future glory.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–15 — Rebuke for neglecting the temple; the people respond
- 2:1–9 — The latter glory greater than the former
- 2:10–19 — Defilement and the promise of blessing
- 2:20–23 — Zerubbabel as God's signet ring

**Christ & significance.** The promise that the latter glory of the temple will exceed the former (2:9) and that Zerubbabel is God's chosen signet (2:23) point to the coming Messiah from David's line who fills God's house with glory. Hebrews 12:26 echoes Haggai's shaking of the heavens and earth.
summary: Haggai, 520 B.C., urges the returned exiles to rebuild the temple. He promises a greater glory and marks Zerubbabel as God's signet ring.

q: Summarize the book of Zechariah: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Zechariah son of Berechiah, a prophet and priest; c. 520–518 B.C. and later, encouraging the post-exilic community in Jerusalem to rebuild and to hope; post-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy / Apocalyptic.

**Theme.** Return to the LORD and hope in the coming King. Through night visions and oracles, Zechariah assures the rebuilders of God's presence and unveils the humble yet triumphant Messiah.

**Outline.**
- 1–6 — Eight night visions calling Israel to return
- 7–8 — Questions about fasting; promises of restoration
- 9–11 — The coming King and the rejected Shepherd
- 12–14 — Jerusalem's deliverance and the LORD's universal reign

**Christ & significance.** Zechariah is richly messianic: the King comes humble on a donkey (9:9, Matthew 21:5), is betrayed for thirty pieces of silver (11:12–13), is the pierced one mourned (12:10, John 19:37), and is the smitten Shepherd (13:7, Matthew 26:31). It points unmistakably to Jesus.
summary: Zechariah, c. 520 B.C., encourages the rebuilders with night visions and rich messianic prophecy—the humble King and the pierced Shepherd.

q: Summarize the book of Malachi: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The prophet Malachi ("my messenger"); c. 433–420 B.C., addressing a spiritually careless post-exilic Judah, likely contemporary with Nehemiah; post-exilic.

**Genre.** Prophecy.

**Theme.** Covenant faithfulness rebuked and renewed. Through a series of disputations, Malachi confronts halfhearted worship, corrupt priests, faithless marriages, and robbed tithes, calling the people back and promising the coming messenger.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–5 — God's electing love for Jacob
- 1:6–2:9 — Corrupt priests and polluted offerings rebuked
- 2:10–16 — Faithlessness in marriage condemned
- 2:17–4:6 — The coming messenger and the Day of the LORD

**Christ & significance.** Malachi promises a messenger to prepare the way and the LORD coming to His temple (3:1), fulfilled by John the Baptist and Christ (Mark 1:2). The closing promise of Elijah's return (4:5–6) is applied to John (Matthew 11:14), closing the Old Testament with anticipation of the Messiah.
summary: Malachi, c. 433–420 B.C., the last Old Testament prophet, rebukes careless post-exilic worship and promises the coming messenger before the Messiah.
